title: Duck typing — if it walks like a duck
instruction:
Python does not require formal interface declarations. If an object has the right methods, it works.

"If it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it is a duck."

Run this. Both `Dog` and `Cat` work with `make_speak()` — no shared parent, no interface declaration. They just both have a `speak()` method.
successMessage: No interface required. Python calls .speak() — if the object has it, it works. This is duck typing: behaviour defined by what an object can do, not what it is.

title: ABC — Abstract Base Class

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Rectangle and Circle both inherit from Shape ABC — both forced to implement area() and perimeter(). If you forget, Python will tell you at instantiation time.

title: ABC prevents instantiation
instruction:
You cannot create an instance of an abstract class — it is only a blueprint.

Run this to see the error.
successMessage: Can't instantiate abstract class Vehicle with abstract method fuel_type. ABCs are templates — define the shape, not the content. Subclasses fill in the blanks.

title: Protocol — structural subtyping
instruction:
`Protocol` is a lighter alternative to ABC. You do not inherit from it — you just need to have the right methods.

```python
from typing import Protocol

class Drawable(Protocol):
    def draw(self) -> str: ...
```

Any class with a `draw()` method satisfies `Drawable` — no inheritance required.

Run this.
successMessage: Neither Circle nor Square inherits from Drawable. They just happen to have draw(). Protocol checks the shape of the object — not its ancestry.

title: @runtime_checkable Protocol
instruction:
By default, Protocols are only checked by type checkers (like mypy), not at runtime.

Add `@runtime_checkable` to make `isinstance()` work at runtime:

```python
@runtime_checkable
class Saveable(Protocol):
    def save(self) -> None: ...
```

Add `@runtime_checkable` and use `isinstance` to check.
starter:
from typing import Protocol, runtime_checkable

# TODO: add @runtime_checkable to this Protocol
class Serializable(Protocol):
    def to_json(self) -> str: ...

class User:
    def to_json(self) -> str:
        return '{"type": "user"}'

class Product:  # no to_json
    pass

u = User()
p = Product()
print(isinstance(u, Serializable))  # should be True
print(isinstance(p, Serializable))  # should be False

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: @runtime_checkable makes isinstance() work with Protocols. Check at runtime whether an object matches the interface without any inheritance.

title: ABC vs Protocol — when to use which
instruction:
Use **ABC** when:
- You want to enforce implementation
- Subclasses need shared base behaviour (`super()` calls)
- You control all the classes

Use **Protocol** when:
- You want flexible duck typing with type safety
- You don't control the classes (third-party, standard library)
- You want structural checking, not inheritance

Fix the code: the `Storable` check should use Protocol (not ABC) because `dict` is a built-in we cannot subclass.
hint: Use Protocol instead of ABC so dict can match it.
successMessage: dict matches Storable without inheriting from it — structural subtyping. ABCs require inheritance; Protocols just require the right methods.

title: Build a plugin system
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a plugin system using Protocol:
- Define `Plugin(Protocol)` with methods `load()` and `process(data: str) -> str`
- Create two plugins: `UpperPlugin` and `ReversePlugin`
- Write a `run_pipeline(plugins, data)` function that applies all plugins in sequence

Test with: `run_pipeline([UpperPlugin(), ReversePlugin()], "hello")`

codeChecks.message: Define process() in the Protocol and both plugins.

successMessage: A Protocol-based plugin pipeline. Add any new plugin without touching run_pipeline — it works with anything that has a process() method. Chapter 18 done.
